headline: How Phoenix is saving lives in the age of deadly heat
meta_description: Phoenix's coordinated approach to extreme heat has reduced deaths from 645 to 405 in two years. Here's what other cities can learn.
summary_short: As record temperatures kill thousands across the globe, Phoenix offers a working blueprint: cooling centres, air conditioning repairs, and a dedicated heat officer.
body:
Heat-related deaths are soaring. France lost over 2,000 people during last summer's record heatwave. England and Wales saw more than 2,700 heat deaths since May. Yet in one American city, the numbers are moving in the opposite direction.

Phoenix, Arizona, has spent years building defences against extreme heat—and it's working. After heat-related deaths peaked at 645 in 2023, they fell to 405 in 2025. The shift comes from systematic, unglamorous work: expanding cooling centres with 24-hour access, offering free air conditioning repairs to eligible residents, and removing people from dangerous streets.

Much of the success traces to a single decision: in 2021, Phoenix became the first city worldwide to hire a dedicated heat officer. "It's incredibly important to make someone responsible for heat," explains Ladd Keith of the University of Arizona's Heat Resilience Initiative. "If it's no one's responsibility, then no one will address it."

Yet the challenge is global. Our bodies need cooler nights to recover from daytime heat, but nighttime temperatures no longer dip as they once did. Scientists warn this is not temporary—it's the new normal.

The good news: Phoenix's blueprint can travel. Other cities can appoint heat officers, establish cooling centres, and coordinate across government departments. What's required is recognising what experts already know: extreme heat is not a summer inconvenience. It's a crisis demanding year-round planning and commitment.
